prompt: An embassy workstation must join the Active Directory domain to receive centrally managed policy and credentials. Which Windows edition CANNOT do this?
rationale: Windows Home cannot join an Active Directory domain; it supports only Workgroup or a personal Microsoft/Entra account sign-in. Pro, Pro for Workstations, and Enterprise can all join a domain, which is why Home is not deployed in managed federal environments.

optionNotes.B: Pro can join an Active Directory domain.
optionNotes.C: Pro for Workstations can join a domain.
optionNotes.D: Enterprise can join a domain and adds further volume-license management features.

reference: Operating Systems › Windows editions & domain join

prompt: A technician needs to start, stop, and change the startup type (Automatic, Manual, Disabled) of Windows services. Which MMC snap-in opens this console directly?
rationale: services.msc opens the Services console, where you set a service's state and its startup type. It is the standard tool for managing background Windows services.

optionNotes.B: devmgmt.msc opens Device Manager for drivers and hardware, not services.
optionNotes.C: diskmgmt.msc opens Disk Management for partitions and volumes.
optionNotes.D: eventvwr.msc opens Event Viewer for logs.

prompt: On a Linux server, which command lists the contents of a directory, including hidden files, in a detailed long format?
rationale: ls lists directory contents; the -l flag gives the long (detailed) listing and -a includes hidden files (those beginning with a dot), so ls -la shows everything with permissions, owner, size, and timestamps.

optionNotes.B: cd changes the working directory; it does not list files.
optionNotes.C: pwd prints the current working directory path, not a file listing.
optionNotes.D: grep searches text for a pattern; -l there lists files that contain a match, not a directory listing.

prompt: Which built-in macOS feature provides automatic, incremental, versioned backups to an external or network disk so files can be restored from an earlier point in time?
rationale: Time Machine is the macOS backup system that saves versioned snapshots to an external or network disk, allowing point-in-time restores of files or the whole system.

optionNotes.B: Gatekeeper enforces that only signed/notarized apps run; it is not a backup tool.
optionNotes.C: FileVault is full-disk encryption, not backup.
optionNotes.D: Spotlight is the system-wide search and quick launcher.

prompt: A technician suspects corrupted Windows system files. Which command scans protected operating-system files and repairs them?
rationale: sfc /scannow runs the System File Checker, which scans all protected Windows system files and replaces corrupted or missing ones with known-good copies.

optionNotes.B: chkdsk /r checks the disk for errors and recovers readable data from bad sectors; it does not repair OS system files specifically.
optionNotes.C: diskpart is the command-line partition manager, not a file-repair tool.
optionNotes.D: ipconfig /flushdns clears the DNS resolver cache.

prompt: A user needs to initialize a brand-new disk, create and format a partition, and assign it a drive letter, all from a graphical console without rebooting. Which tool is designed for this?
rationale: Disk Management (diskmgmt.msc) initializes new disks (choosing GPT or MBR), creates/deletes/formats partitions, assigns drive letters, and extends or shrinks volumes, with most operations requiring no reboot.

optionNotes.B: Device Manager manages hardware and drivers, not partitions.
optionNotes.C: Performance Monitor tracks performance counters and data collector sets.
optionNotes.D: Task Scheduler runs programs on triggers or schedules.

prompt: A shared folder on an NTFS volume has Share permission set to Read for the Marketing group, while the NTFS permission for that group is Modify. What effective access does a Marketing user get when reaching the folder OVER THE NETWORK?
rationale: For network access, the effective permission is the MORE RESTRICTIVE (intersection) of the share permission and the NTFS permission. Share Read combined with NTFS Modify yields Read, because Read is the more restrictive of the two.

optionNotes.B: Modify is the NTFS level, but the more restrictive Share Read caps network access at Read.
optionNotes.C: Full Control is granted by neither permission set here.
optionNotes.D: Both sets grant at least Read, so access is not denied entirely.

reference: Operating Systems › NTFS vs share permissions

prompt: An administrator wants to perform a clean-boot diagnosis by enabling Safe Boot and selectively disabling services at startup. Which built-in tool provides these boot and service options?
rationale: msconfig (System Configuration) controls boot options such as Safe Boot, boot logging, and processor count, and lets you selectively disable services for clean-boot troubleshooting. Note that startup PROGRAM management has moved to Task Manager.

optionNotes.B: msinfo32 is a read-only inventory of hardware and the software environment; it changes nothing.
optionNotes.C: dxdiag reports DirectX, display, and sound diagnostics only.
optionNotes.D: regedit edits the registry directly but is not the boot-configuration tool.

prompt: A USB flash drive must be used to transfer a single 6 GB video file between Windows, macOS, and Linux machines with native read/write on all three. Which file system best meets this need?
rationale: exFAT removes FAT32's 4 GB per-file limit and is natively read/write on Windows, macOS, and Linux, making it ideal for large files on cross-platform removable media.

optionNotes.B: FAT32 has a 4 GB maximum file size, so a 6 GB file will not fit.
optionNotes.C: NTFS is native read/write on Windows but typically read-only on macOS without extra software.
optionNotes.D: ext4 is a Linux file system and is not natively writable on Windows or macOS.

prompt: On a Linux host, an administrator must install a package on an Ubuntu server and on a separate Red Hat Enterprise Linux server. Which package managers are the correct native choices, respectively?

options.text: apt for Ubuntu; yum or dnf for RHEL
yum for Ubuntu; apt for RHEL
pacman for Ubuntu; zypper for RHEL
dnf for Ubuntu; pacman for RHEL

rationale: Debian/Ubuntu use APT with .deb packages, while Red Hat/Fedora/CentOS use RPM packages managed by yum or the newer dnf. Matching the manager to the distribution family is essential.

optionNotes.B: This reverses the families; yum is Red Hat and apt is Debian/Ubuntu.
optionNotes.C: pacman belongs to Arch and zypper to SUSE; neither is the Ubuntu or RHEL default.
optionNotes.D: dnf is Red Hat/Fedora, not Ubuntu; pacman is Arch, not RHEL.

prompt: A program is hung and unresponsive. Using Task Manager, which tab lets a technician select the application and click End Task to force it to close?
rationale: The Processes tab lists running apps and background processes with live resource usage and provides the End Task button to terminate a hung program.

optionNotes.B: The Performance tab graphs CPU, memory, disk, network, and GPU usage; it does not end individual apps.
optionNotes.C: The Startup tab enables or disables programs that launch at boot.
optionNotes.D: The Users tab shows logged-on users and their resource consumption.

prompt: After editing domain Group Policy, an administrator wants a workstation to reapply all policy immediately rather than wait for the next refresh. Which command does this?
rationale: gpupdate /force forces an immediate refresh and reapplies all Group Policy settings, not just changed ones, to the computer and user.

optionNotes.B: gpresult /r reports the Resultant Set of Policy already applied; it does not trigger a refresh.
optionNotes.C: gpedit.msc opens the Local Group Policy Editor to edit policy, not to refresh it.
optionNotes.D: sfc /scannow repairs system files and is unrelated to Group Policy.

prompt: A PowerShell user wants to retrieve a list of running processes and act on their properties in the pipeline. Which statement about the correct cmdlet and PowerShell's pipeline is accurate?

options.text: Get-Process returns process objects, and PowerShell passes .NET objects (not plain text) down the pipeline
Process-Get returns text, and PowerShell passes only plain text down the pipeline
Get-Process returns text strings that must be parsed before filtering
List-Process returns objects, but the pipeline discards their properties

rationale: PowerShell cmdlets use a Verb-Noun form, so the correct cmdlet is Get-Process, and unlike CMD it passes structured .NET objects through the pipeline, letting you filter and act on properties directly.

optionNotes.B: The cmdlet is Get-Process, not Process-Get, and PowerShell does not pass plain text like CMD.
optionNotes.C: Get-Process returns objects with properties, so no text parsing is required.
optionNotes.D: The valid cmdlet is Get-Process, and the pipeline preserves object properties rather than discarding them.

prompt: During a Windows installation on a UEFI system with a GPT-partitioned disk, which file system is used to format the EFI System Partition that holds the bootloader?
rationale: On a UEFI/GPT Windows install, the setup creates an EFI System Partition formatted FAT32, because UEFI firmware reads its bootloaders from a FAT-formatted ESP.

optionNotes.B: NTFS is used for the main Windows system volume, not the EFI System Partition.
optionNotes.C: ext4 is a Linux file system and is not used for the Windows ESP.
optionNotes.D: exFAT is for removable media and is not the ESP format.

prompt: A folder's Share permission is Full Control for Everyone, but its NTFS permission denies the Contractors group Write (they have only Read allowed). A contractor connects to the share over the network and tries to save a file. What is the result, and why?

options.text: The save fails; the effective network permission is the most restrictive combination, and NTFS limits the contractor to Read
The save succeeds because Share Full Control overrides the NTFS setting
The save succeeds because the most permissive of the two always wins
The save fails because share permissions always take precedence over NTFS

rationale: Over the network, effective access is the intersection (most restrictive) of share and NTFS permissions. Share grants Full Control, but NTFS allows only Read for contractors, so the resulting access is Read and the write is denied.

optionNotes.B: Share permissions do not override NTFS; the two are combined and the stricter one wins.
optionNotes.C: For network access the MOST restrictive, not the most permissive, of the two applies.
optionNotes.D: Share permissions do not automatically take precedence; the more restrictive of share and NTFS governs.

reference: Operating Systems › NTFS vs share permissions

prompt: An administrator plans to deploy a 4 TB boot disk to many identical workstations and wants the maximum supported partitions and resilient partition-table backups. Which firmware and partition-scheme combination is required?

options.text: UEFI with GPT, which supports disks well over 2 TB and stores backup copies of the partition table
Legacy BIOS with MBR, which supports up to 4 primary partitions and disks over 4 TB
Legacy BIOS with GPT, the only combination that boots a 4 TB disk
UEFI with MBR, which supports 128 partitions and a backup table

rationale: GPT supports disks far larger than 2 TB, up to 128 partitions in Windows, and keeps backup copies of the partition table for resilience; booting GPT for Windows requires UEFI firmware. This is the correct choice for a 4 TB boot disk.

optionNotes.B: MBR is limited to a 2 TB disk and 4 primary partitions, so it cannot boot a 4 TB disk.
optionNotes.C: Windows GPT boot requires UEFI, not legacy BIOS, so this combination does not boot.
optionNotes.D: The 128-partition limit and backup table are GPT features, not MBR; MBR allows only 4 primary partitions.

reference: Operating Systems › OS installation/boot (UEFI/GPT vs BIOS/MBR)

prompt: A help-desk script must terminate a frozen application by its process ID at the Windows command prompt, force-closing it even if it ignores a normal close request. Which command is correct?

options.text: taskkill /PID 4820 /T only, with no force option

rationale: taskkill ends a process; /PID specifies the target by process ID and /F forces termination even if the program does not respond to a normal close request. (tasklist is used first to find the PID.)

optionNotes.B: tasklist only lists running processes and their PIDs; it cannot terminate a process.
optionNotes.C: /IM expects an image name (e.g., app.exe), not a numeric PID, and /S specifies a remote system rather than forcing termination.
optionNotes.D: /T ends a process tree but without /F it sends a normal close; it will not force-kill an unresponsive app.

prompt: On a Linux system, a script file deploy.sh is owned by root and must be made readable, writable, and executable by the owner, and read+execute by group and others, then run with elevated privileges by a non-root admin. Which pair of commands achieves exactly this?
rationale: chmod 755 sets rwx for the owner and r-x for group and others, exactly as described, and sudo runs the script with elevated (root) privileges for a single command. This matches the requirement precisely.

optionNotes.B: chmod 777 grants write to group and others (rwx for all), which exceeds the requirement, and su without arguments switches to the root shell rather than running one command.
optionNotes.C: chmod 644 sets rw-r--r-- with no execute bit, so the script cannot be executed.
optionNotes.D: chown changes ownership, not permission bits, so it does not set the requested rwx/r-x mode.
